planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 9b–11; Satin Leaf is a small to medium native evergreen tree of the Sapotaceae family — native to South Florida hammocks, the Caribbean, and Central America — famous for leaves with a lustrous coppery-gold silky underside that flash dramatically when breezes turn them over, producing small, purple-black, edible Sapotaceae fruits similar in family to Star Apple and Mamey Sapote.

directions: Plant Satin Leaf in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; water regularly during establishment; mature trees tolerate brief cold to 28°F; protect young plants from hard frost; no significant pruning required. Chrysophyllum oliviforme is among South Florida's most striking native trees, instantly recognized by the extraordinary bicolor leaves — dark glossy green above and covered with dense, copper-golden, silky hairs below — that shimmer and catch light dramatically when wind moves through the canopy. The tree grows in South Florida's tropical hardwood hammocks alongside Gumbo Limbo, Strangler Fig, and Pigeon Plum, and is a highly recommended native alternative to non-native ornamental trees for South Florida landscapes. Small clusters of tiny white flowers give rise to small, dark purple, oval fruits (less than 1 inch) — technically edible and sweet when fully ripe, similar in structure to small star apples (in the same family), though the thin flesh makes them a minor snack rather than a major crop. The fruits are enthusiastically consumed by mockingbirds, robins, and migrating warblers in winter. An excellent shade tree for South Florida native gardens, providing year-round color interest through the glinting copper undersides of its foliage.
sources: Wunderlin, R.P. & Hansen, B.F. — Flora of Florida
Gilman, E.F. & Watson, D.G. — 'Chrysophyllum oliviforme', USDA Forest Service Fact Sheet
Florida Native Plant Society — Chrysophyllum oliviforme

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in deep, fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 10–12; Taraw Palm is a tall, stately, single-trunk fan palm native to lowland forests, riverbanks, and coastal zones of the Philippines, Vietnam, Cambodia, Indonesia, and Papua New Guinea — one of the tallest and most impressive Livistona species — producing large drooping skirts of deeply divided fan leaves on a robust gray-brown trunk, with clusters of small, blue-black, edible fruits used locally in traditional food preparations.

directions: Plant Taraw Palm in deep, fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; water regularly during establishment and in dry seasons; young plants tolerate partial shade for the first few years; mature palms are self-cleaning (old fronds drop cleanly) and require no pruning. Livistona saribus is among the larger and more majestic members of the Livistona genus — the widespread Asian fan palm group represented in cultivation by the Chinese Fan Palm (L. chinensis) and Australian Fan Palm (L. australis). The Taraw Palm develops a single straight gray-brown trunk up to 40–80 feet tall, topped by a graceful crown of large, deeply divided fan leaves whose drooping tips give the crown a characteristic pendulous, elegant character. A fast grower by palm standards in good conditions, it is widely used as a landscape palm in Southeast Asian cities and is gaining favor in zone 10–12 tropical and subtropical gardens globally. In its native range, the leaves are traditionally harvested for weaving baskets, mats, and hats; the blue-black clustered fruits are eaten by local wildlife and occasionally by people as a minor food. An excellent large specimen palm for tropical gardens where a dramatic, fountain-crowned palm silhouette is desired.
sources: Dransfield, J. et al. — 'Genera Palmarum: The Evolution and Classification of Palms' (2008)

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade to full shade in deep, fertile, moist, humus-rich, acidic soil with high humidity in zone 11–12; Stilt Palm (Seychelles Stilt Palm) is a single-trunk palm endemic to the steep, humid forests of the granitic Seychelles islands — one of the botanical jewels of the Seychelles — unique among palms for its conspicuous mass of aerial stilt roots supporting the lower trunk above the forest floor, producing simple (undivided), large pinnate-ribbed leaves and a dramatically exotic appearance in the shaded tropical garden.

directions: Plant Stilt Palm in humus-rich, moist, well-drained, acidic soil in partial to deep shade with year-round high humidity; maintain consistently moist soil; never expose to frost or dry air; protect from direct sun, which scorches the leaves. Verschaffeltia splendida is among the world's most botanically distinctive palms — endemic to the granitic Seychelles islands and found naturally in the steep, humid, shaded ravines and forest slopes of Mahé and Praslin. Its most striking feature is the prominent mass of aerial stilt roots that emerge from the lower trunk and arch outward to anchor the palm in the steep, leaf-litter-covered forest slopes — an adaptation to the unstable, steep hillside habitats it occupies, allowing the palm to maintain stability while the main trunk rises straight and slender above. The leaves are entire (undivided) when young, becoming irregularly split with age in a distinctive pattern not seen in most palms. Reaching 20–40 feet at maturity, with a smooth, slender gray trunk rising dramatically above the radiating stilt root mass, the Stilt Palm is one of the most sought-after and visually extraordinary palms for specialist tropical collectors and botanical garden palm collections. It requires consistent high humidity, shade, and moist soil year-round — a demanding palm for zone 12 conditions only, but extraordinarily rewarding.
sources: Seychelles Islands Foundation — Flora of the Seychelles
